data.title: Has The Market Gone Too Far On Diageo (LSE:DGE) After Multi Year Share Price Weakness?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Diageo’s current share price reflects its long term potential, the key question is what you are actually paying for today.
  • The stock recently closed at £14.71, with returns of 2.3% over 7 days and 1.4% over 30 days, set against a year to date return of an 8.5% decline and a 1 year return of a 26.5% decline.
  • Over a 3 year period the share price return sits at a 55.9% decline and over 5 years at a 47.7% decline. This gives important context for anyone assessing whether sentiment has swung too far. These multi year returns frame the question of whether current pricing reflects temporary pessimism or a reset in expectations.
  • Simply Wall St’s valuation model gives Diageo a score of 4 out of 6. The rest of this article will break that down using different valuation approaches before finishing with a way to assess value that goes beyond any single metric.

Find out why Diageo's -26.5% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Approach 1: Diageo Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting them back to today’s value. It focuses on the cash that could be available to shareholders, rather than accounting earnings.

For Diageo, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about US$2.58b. Analyst estimates and subsequent extrapolations by Simply Wall St suggest free cash flow projections rising into the low US$3b range from 2026, with a forecast of US$3.89b in 2030. Ten year projections combine analyst inputs for the earlier years with more mechanical growth estimates thereafter.

When those future cash flows are discounted back, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of US$28.27 per share. Compared with the recent share price of £14.71, this implies the stock is 48.0% undervalued based on this DCF framework.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Diageo is undervalued by 48.0%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 8 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Diageo.

Approach 2: Diageo Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is often a useful way to think about value because it links what you pay for each share to the earnings that business is currently generating. The level of P/E that might be considered normal or fair usually reflects what the market thinks about a company’s growth prospects and how risky those earnings are, with higher expected growth and lower perceived risk often supporting higher multiples.

Diageo’s current P/E is 18.4x, compared with an average of 16.8x for peers in its group and 17.1x for the Beverage industry. Simply Wall St also calculates a Fair Ratio of 23.5x for Diageo, which is the P/E that might be expected given factors such as its earnings profile, industry, profit margins, market value and risk characteristics. This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a straight comparison with peers or the industry because it adjusts for company specific traits rather than assuming all businesses deserve the same multiple. Compared with this Fair Ratio, Diageo’s actual 18.4x P/E sits below that level, which points to the shares trading on a lower multiple than this framework would imply.

Result: UNDERVALUED

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today using a required rate of return. The idea is simple: what matters is the cash the business can return to shareholders over time, expressed in today’s dollars.

For Dutch Bros, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flows reported and projected in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $4.0 million. Analyst estimates and extrapolations point to free cash flow of $194.9 million by 2029, with additional projections out to 2035 provided by Simply Wall St’s model.

When all of those projected cash flows are discounted back and summed, the DCF output is an intrinsic value of about $33.63 per share, compared with a recent share price around $51.72. On this basis, the model suggests Dutch Bros is about 53.8% overvalued using these inputs and assumptions.

Dutch Bros currently trades on a P/E of about 82.46x. That sits above both the Hospitality industry average of around 21.58x and the peer group average of about 61.26x. This signals that the market is paying a richer price for each dollar of current earnings than for many similar stocks.

This AI rollout is aimed at integrating tools directly into daily workflows and coding processes, rather than existing as a separate app. For readers tracking how large banks are using AI, the emphasis on governance, data ethics and real operational use cases makes Scotia Intelligence an important development to watch over time.

📰 Beyond the headline: 1 risk and 4 things going right for Bank of Nova Scotia that every investor should see.

For Bank of Nova Scotia, Scotia Intelligence looks less like a flashy front end and more like core plumbing for how the bank works day to day. AI is already handling over 40% of contact centre queries and routing about 90% of commercial emails, which directly targets two big pressure points for large banks: operating costs and service speed. If these tools keep freeing up staff time for higher value work, that could support efforts to improve efficiency, client retention and cross-selling, areas where Scotiabank competes with peers such as Royal Bank of Canada, TD Bank and Bank of Montreal.

Preferred Price to Book Multiple of 2.7x: Is It Justified?

On a P/B basis, NioCorp trades at 2.7x, which sits below both its direct peer average of 9.5x and the broader US metals and mining average of 3x.

The P/B ratio compares the company’s market value to the book value of its net assets. This can be a useful reference point for asset heavy businesses with limited current earnings. For a pre revenue, loss making developer like NioCorp, this tends to reflect how much investors are willing to pay today for its Elk Creek project and other assets relative to their current accounting value.

Given NioCorp’s lack of meaningful revenue, current net loss of $56.108m and the fact that earnings have declined by 31.3% per year over the past 5 years, the relatively low P/B multiple indicates the market is assigning a more conservative price tag than peers that already have stronger earnings profiles. At the same time, the P/B level still sits quite close to the wider industry average, which shows investors are not heavily discounting it compared with other US metals and mining names.

Against the sector, the contrast is clear. Direct peers on average trade at a P/B of 9.5x, more than three times NioCorp’s 2.7x, while the broader US metals and mining group stands at 3x. That gap to peers points to a materially lower valuation being applied to NioCorp’s balance sheet, even though it is roughly in line with the industry overall.

Approach 1: Gates Industrial Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today’s value. It focuses on the cash the business is expected to generate for shareholders, not just current earnings.

The latest twelve month free cash flow is reported as a loss of about $42.3 million, so the model leans heavily on expected future free cash flow rather than recent figures. Analyst and extrapolated estimates suggest free cash flow reaching about $3.3 billion in 2035, with intermediate projections such as $2.0 billion in 2026 and $2.6 billion in 2028, all discounted back to today using the DCF framework.

On this basis, the estimated intrinsic value comes out at roughly $310.17 per share, compared with the current share price of about $194.11. That implies a 37.4% discount, which indicates that Steel Dynamics is trading below this model-based estimate of fair value.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Steel Dynamics is undervalued by 37.4%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 60 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Steel Dynamics.

Approach 2: Steel Dynamics Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful guide because it links what you pay for the stock to the earnings the business is currently generating. The higher the expected earnings growth and the lower the perceived risk, the more investors are often willing to pay in terms of a higher P/E multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually supports a lower, more conservative P/E.

Steel Dynamics currently trades on a P/E of 23.7x. This sits above the peer group average of 20.2x and also above the broader Metals and Mining industry average of 22.9x. This suggests the market is applying a somewhat richer earnings multiple than these simple benchmarks. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Steel Dynamics is 27.4x, which is the P/E level implied by its model after factoring in elements such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market size and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a straight peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for those underlying differences rather than assuming all companies deserve the same multiple. Since the Fair Ratio of 27.4x is above the current P/E of 23.7x, this approach suggests the shares may be trading below that model based earnings multiple.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Steel Dynamics Narrative

Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. Narratives on Simply Wall St let you set out your story for Steel Dynamics, link that story to your own revenue, earnings and margin estimates, convert those into a Fair Value, and then compare that Fair Value with the current price in a simple tool on the Community page that updates when new information such as earnings or tariff news arrives. For example, one investor might build a narrative around strong infrastructure demand, 6.8% forecast revenue growth, an 8.3% profit margin and a Fair Value close to the current analyst consensus of US$188.25. Another might focus on risks like industry overcapacity and pressure from new investments and arrive at a lower Fair Value, with each Narrative giving a clear, price-anchored framework for deciding whether the stock currently fits their own view.
